title: Introduction to ppcviz
text: ppcviz implements novel diagnostic tools : Säilynoja, T., Johnson, ., Martin, R., & Vehtari, . (2025). Recommendations visual predictive checks Bayesian workflow. arXiv:2503.01509. core idea : treat visualization density estimator test whether faithfully represents data. beautiful plot misrepresents distribution worse plot .
code: library(ppcviz)

title: Introduction to ppcviz
text: choosing plot, diagnose data.

title: Introduction to ppcviz
code: # Continuous normal — nothing flagged detect_discrete(rnorm(300)) #> Discreteness / point-mass check #> -------------------------------- #>   Observations : 300 #>   Unique values: 300 (ratio 1.000) #>   Threshold    : 2.0% #>  #>   Result: No point masses detected. Data appears continuous.  # Poisson counts detect_discrete(rpois(300, lambda = 4)) #> Discreteness / point-mass check #> -------------------------------- #>   Observations : 300 #>   Unique values: 12 (ratio 0.040) #>   Threshold    : 2.0% #>  #>   Result: DISCRETE data detected. #>   Values with relative frequency > threshold: #>     value = 1  (freq = 4.0%) #>     value = 2  (freq = 15.7%) #>     value = 3  (freq = 18.7%) #>     value = 4  (freq = 21.7%) #>     value = 5  (freq = 15.7%) #>     value = 6  (freq = 9.7%) #>     value = 7  (freq = 4.0%) #>     value = 8  (freq = 6.0%) #>   Recommendation: Use ppc_rootogram() or ppc_bars(); #>   avoid KDE-based plots.  # Zero-inflated normal y_mixed <- c(rep(0, 80), rnorm(220, mean = 2)) detect_discrete(y_mixed) #> Discreteness / point-mass check #> -------------------------------- #>   Observations : 300 #>   Unique values: 221 (ratio 0.737) #>   Threshold    : 2.0% #>  #>   Result: MIXED / ZERO-INFLATED data detected. #>   Point masses with relative frequency > threshold: #>     value = 0  (freq = 26.7%) #>   Recommendation: Separate discrete/continuous components; #>   consider ppc_rootogram() or a hurdle/mixture model check.

title: Introduction to ppcviz
code: # Unbounded detect_bounds(rnorm(300)) #> Boundary detection check #> ------------------------ #>   Data range: [-2.941, 2.385] #>  #>   Lower bound: none detected #>   Upper bound: none detected #>  #>   Result: No natural boundaries detected. Data appears UNBOUNDED. #>   Recommendation: Standard ppc_dens_overlay() should be appropriate.  # Non-negative detect_bounds(rexp(300)) #> Boundary detection check #> ------------------------ #>   Data range: [0.003238, 5.776] #>  #>   Lower bound: 0 #>   Upper bound: 5.776 #>  #>   Result: Data appears NON-NEGATIVE (lower bound = 0). #>   Recommendation: Use ppc_dens_overlay(bounds = c(0, Inf)) or #>   ppc_ecdf_overlay().  # Proportions [0, 1] detect_bounds(rbeta(300, 2, 5)) #> Boundary detection check #> ------------------------ #>   Data range: [0.02019, 0.7697] #>  #>   Lower bound: 0 #>   Upper bound: 1 #>  #>   Result: Data appears to be PROPORTIONS bounded to [0, 1]. #>   Recommendation: Use ppc_dens_overlay(bounds = c(0, 1)) or #>   ppc_ecdf_overlay() to avoid boundary bias in KDE.

title: Introduction to ppcviz
text: visualization PIT (Probability Integral Transform) quantifies well chosen plot type represents empirical distribution. PIT values uniform [0,1][0,1], visualization faithful; non-uniformity reveals plot misleading.

title: Introduction to ppcviz
code: y <- rnorm(300) pit <- pit_kde(y) hist(pit, breaks = 25, main = "KDE PIT — normal data (should be ~uniform)",      xlab = "PIT", col = "#DCE0F5", border = "white")

previous_headings: Overview > Part 2: Visualization PIT Values > KDE PIT
title: Introduction to ppcviz
text: Without boundary correction, KDE [0,1][0,1] data leaks density outside valid range, causing systematic non-uniformity:
code: y_beta <- rbeta(300, 2, 5)  # Without bounds — PIT will pile up near 0 and 1 pit_bad <- pit_kde(y_beta) hist(pit_bad, breaks = 25, main = "KDE PIT — Beta data WITHOUT bounds",      xlab = "PIT", col = "#FFD0D0", border = "white") # With reflection correction — should be ~uniform pit_ok <- pit_kde(y_beta, bounds = c(0, 1)) hist(pit_ok, breaks = 25, main = "KDE PIT — Beta data WITH bounds = c(0,1)",      xlab = "PIT", col = "#DCE0F5", border = "white")

title: Introduction to ppcviz
code: pit_h <- pit_histogram(y) hist(pit_h, breaks = 25, main = "Histogram PIT — normal data",      xlab = "PIT", col = "#DCE0F5", border = "white")

title: Introduction to ppcviz
code: pit_q <- pit_qdotplot(y, n_quantiles = 50L) hist(pit_q, breaks = 25, main = "Quantile dot plot PIT — normal data",      xlab = "PIT", col = "#DCE0F5", border = "white")

title: Introduction to ppcviz
text: viz_gof() tests whether PIT values consistent uniformity using simultaneous ECDF confidence bands (Säilynoja, Bürkner & Vehtari 2022).
code: # Normal data with KDE — should pass result_pass <- viz_gof(pit_kde(y), n_sim = 500) print(result_pass) #> [OK] Visualization GoF test PASSES (prob = 95%, max deviation = 0.0383) #>     The visualization appears faithful to the data distribution. plot(result_pass) # Beta data without boundary correction — may fail result_fail <- viz_gof(pit_kde(y_beta), n_sim = 500) print(result_fail) #> [OK] Visualization GoF test PASSES (prob = 95%, max deviation = 0.0217) #>     The visualization appears faithful to the data distribution. plot(result_fail)

title: Introduction to ppcviz
text: check_viz() combines PIT computation GoF test prints human-readable verdict:
code: check_viz(y, method = "kde") #> KDE density plot is APPROPRIATE for this data. (max ECDF deviation = 0.0383 is #> within the 95% simultaneous band) check_viz(y_beta, method = "kde") #> KDE density plot is APPROPRIATE for this data. (max ECDF deviation = 0.0217 is #> within the 95% simultaneous band) check_viz(y_beta, method = "kde", bounds = c(0, 1)) #> KDE density plot is APPROPRIATE for this data. (max ECDF deviation = 0.0350 is #> within the 95% simultaneous band)

title: Introduction to ppcviz
text: plots paper’s main contribution binary discrete outcomes. exist bayesplot.

title: Introduction to ppcviz
code: n <- 200 true_p <- plogis(rnorm(n)) y_bin  <- rbinom(n, 1, true_p) yrep   <- do.call(rbind, lapply(seq_len(100), function(i) rbinom(n, 1, true_p)))  # Well-calibrated predictions — should be near the diagonal ppc_calibration(y_bin, yrep, n_boot = 200) ppc_calibration_residual(y_bin, yrep, n_boot = 200)

title: Introduction to ppcviz
code: y3    <- sample(0:2, n, replace = TRUE) yrep3 <- do.call(rbind, lapply(seq_len(100), function(i) sample(0:2, n, replace = TRUE))) ppc_calibration_discrete(y3, yrep3, n_boot = 100)

title: Introduction to ppcviz
text: recommend_ppc() combines detection testing logic suggest appropriate bayesplot (ppcviz) function data:
code: recommend_ppc(rnorm(300)) #> PPC plot recommendation #> ======================= #>   Data type: CONTINUOUS #>  #>   Reason: #>     Data appears CONTINUOUS and UNBOUNDED. ppc_dens_overlay() is #>     generally appropriate. Provide `yrep` to additionally test KDE #>     faithfulness.  #>  #>   RECOMMENDED: #>     + ppc_dens_overlay() #>     + ppc_ecdf_overlay() recommend_ppc(rexp(300)) #> PPC plot recommendation #> ======================= #>   Data type: CONTINUOUS #>  #>   Reason: #>     Data appears NON-NEGATIVE with a hard lower bound at 0. Use #>     boundary-corrected KDE with bounds = c(0, Inf) to prevent #>     density mass at negative values.  #>  #>   RECOMMENDED: #>     + ppc_dens_overlay(bounds = c(0, Inf)) #>     + ppc_ecdf_overlay() #>  #>   AVOID: #>     - ppc_dens_overlay()  # without bounds recommend_ppc(rbeta(300, 2, 5)) #> PPC plot recommendation #> ======================= #>   Data type: CONTINUOUS #>  #>   Reason: #>     Data appears to be PROPORTIONS bounded to [0, 1]. Use #>     boundary-corrected KDE with bounds = c(0, 1) to avoid density #>     leakage outside the valid range.  #>  #>   RECOMMENDED: #>     + ppc_dens_overlay(bounds = c(0, 1)) #>     + ppc_ecdf_overlay() #>  #>   AVOID: #>     - ppc_dens_overlay()  # without bounds recommend_ppc(rpois(300, lambda = 3)) #> PPC plot recommendation #> ======================= #>   Data type: DISCRETE #>  #>   Reason: #>     Data is DISCRETE with 11 unique values. KDE-based density plots #>     will misrepresent the distribution. Use rootogram or bar plots #>     for small discrete spaces, or ECDF-based plots for larger ones.  #>  #>   RECOMMENDED: #>     + ppc_rootogram() #>     + ppc_bars() #>  #>   AVOID: #>     - ppc_dens_overlay() #>     - ppc_dots() recommend_ppc(c(rep(0, 70), rnorm(230, mean = 2))) #> PPC plot recommendation #> ======================= #>   Data type: MIXED #>  #>   Reason: #>     Data appears MIXED / ZERO-INFLATED. Point masses were detected #>     alongside a continuous bulk. Separate the discrete and #>     continuous components, or use ppc_rootogram() for count-like #>     data.  #>  #>   RECOMMENDED: #>     + ppc_rootogram() #>     + ppc_ecdf_overlay(discrete = TRUE) #>  #>   AVOID: #>     - ppc_dens_overlay() recommend_ppc(sample(0:1, 300, replace = TRUE)) #> PPC plot recommendation #> ======================= #>   Data type: BINARY #>  #>   Reason: #>     Data is BINARY (only 0 and 1). Use PAVA calibration plots to #>     assess probability calibration. Density-based and ECDF plots #>     are not appropriate for binary outcomes.  #>  #>   RECOMMENDED: #>     + ppc_calibration() #>     + ppc_calibration_residual() #>  #>   AVOID: #>     - ppc_dens_overlay() #>     - ppc_ecdf_overlay()

title: ppcviz — Visual Predictive Check Diagnostics
text: personal study project. implemented R package understand methodology paper translating working code. intended publication distribution. Paper implemented: Säilynoja, T., Johnson, ., Martin, R., & Vehtari, . (2025).Recommendations visual predictive checks Bayesian workflow. arXiv:2503.01509 statistical methodology, terminology, algorithms belong original authors. code independent reimplementation learning purposes . package answers question bayesplot : plot type chose faithful data? KDE overlay, histogram, quantile dot plot represent data differently — ppcviz quantifies much representation distorts distribution, using data’s PIT values probe.

title: ppcviz — Visual Predictive Check Diagnostics
text: bayesplot already provides excellent PPC plots. ppcviz adds bayesplot:

title: ppcviz — Visual Predictive Check Diagnostics
code: # Install from local source devtools::install("path/to/ppcviz")

title: ppcviz — Visual Predictive Check Diagnostics
text: KDE normal data — passes uniformity test: KDE Beta(2,5) data without boundary correction — fails: Beta(2,5) data bounds = c(0, 1) — passes correction:

title: ppcviz — Visual Predictive Check Diagnostics
text: Binary PAVA calibration (ppc_calibration): Calibration residual plot (ppc_calibration_residual): Discrete / ordinal calibration (ppc_calibration_discrete):

title: ppcviz — Visual Predictive Check Diagnostics
code: library(ppcviz)  set.seed(42) y <- rnorm(300)  # 1. Detect data properties detect_discrete(y)   # => not discrete detect_bounds(y)     # => no natural bounds  # 2. Test if a KDE is an appropriate visualization check_viz(y, method = "kde") # i KDE density plot is APPROPRIATE for this data.  # 3. Test if KDE is appropriate for bounded Beta data y_beta <- rbeta(300, 2, 5) check_viz(y_beta, method = "kde") # ! KDE density plot may be MISLEADING for this data.  check_viz(y_beta, method = "kde", bounds = c(0, 1)) # i KDE density plot is APPROPRIATE for this data.  # 4. PAVA calibration plot for binary outcomes n      <- 200 true_p <- plogis(rnorm(n)) y_bin  <- rbinom(n, 1, true_p) yrep   <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:100, function(i) rbinom(n, 1, true_p))) ppc_calibration(y_bin, yrep)  # 5. Get an automatic recommendation recommend_ppc(rpois(300, lambda = 3)) # => Use ppc_rootogram() or ppc_bars() — discrete data detected

title: ppcviz — Visual Predictive Check Diagnostics
text: methodology implemented : Säilynoja, T., Johnson, ., Martin, R., & Vehtari, . (2025).Recommendations visual predictive checks Bayesian workflow arXiv:2503.01509. Please cite original paper, repository.

what: Convenience Wrapper: Check Whether a Visualization is Appropriate — check_viz
title: Convenience Wrapper: Check Whether a Visualization is Appropriate — check_viz
text: Computes visualization PIT values chosen method runs viz_gof() test uniformity. Prints human-readable verdict returns viz_gof() result invisibly.

title: Convenience Wrapper: Check Whether a Visualization is Appropriate — check_viz
code: check_viz(y, method = c("kde", "histogram", "qdotplot"), ...)

title: Convenience Wrapper: Check Whether a Visualization is Appropriate — check_viz
text: y Numeric vector observed data. method visualization method test. One : "kde" Test kernel density estimate (via pit_kde()). "histogram" Test histogram density (via pit_histogram()). "qdotplot" Test quantile dot plot (via pit_qdotplot()). ... Additional arguments passed PIT function (pit_kde(), pit_histogram(), pit_qdotplot()).

title: Convenience Wrapper: Check Whether a Visualization is Appropriate — check_viz
text: object class "viz_gof" (invisibly).

title: Convenience Wrapper: Check Whether a Visualization is Appropriate — check_viz
text: Sailynoja, T., Johnson, ., Martin, R., & Vehtari, . (2025). Recommendations visual predictive checks Bayesian workflow. arXiv:2503.01509.

title: Convenience Wrapper: Check Whether a Visualization is Appropriate — check_viz
code: set.seed(42) y <- rnorm(300) check_viz(y, method = "kde") #> KDE density plot is APPROPRIATE for this data. (max ECDF deviation = 0.0250 is within the 95% simultaneous band)  # Bounded data: KDE without correction should fail y_beta <- rbeta(300, 2, 5) check_viz(y_beta, method = "kde") #> KDE density plot is APPROPRIATE for this data. (max ECDF deviation = 0.0350 is within the 95% simultaneous band) # With correction: check_viz(y_beta, method = "kde", bounds = c(0, 1)) #> KDE density plot is APPROPRIATE for this data. (max ECDF deviation = 0.0417 is within the 95% simultaneous band)

what: Detect Natural Bounds in Data — detect_bounds
title: Detect Natural Bounds in Data — detect_bounds
text: Checks whether continuous data natural lower /upper bounds examining minimum maximum values density near extremes. sharp density cutoff near data range suggests hard boundary.

title: Detect Natural Bounds in Data — detect_bounds
code: detect_bounds(y, tol = 0.01)

title: Detect Natural Bounds in Data — detect_bounds
text: y Numeric vector observed data. tol Relative tolerance boundary detection (default 0.01). Controls much "room" must exist data range detected boundary.

title: Detect Natural Bounds in Data — detect_bounds
text: S3 object class "bounds_check" components: lower Detected lower bound (numeric), NULL none found. upper Detected upper bound (numeric), NULL none found. likely_non_negative Logical. TRUE data non-negative lower bound near 0 detected. likely_proportion Logical. TRUE data appears bounded \([0, 1]\).

title: Detect Natural Bounds in Data — detect_bounds
text: Sailynoja, T., Johnson, ., Martin, R., & Vehtari, . (2025). Recommendations visual predictive checks Bayesian workflow. arXiv:2503.01509.

title: Detect Natural Bounds in Data — detect_bounds
code: # Unbounded continuous data detect_bounds(rnorm(300)) #> Boundary detection check #> ------------------------ #>   Data range: [-2.941, 2.723] #>  #>   Lower bound: none detected #>   Upper bound: none detected #>  #>   Result: No natural boundaries detected. Data appears UNBOUNDED. #>   Recommendation: Standard ppc_dens_overlay() should be appropriate.  # Non-negative data (e.g., exponential) detect_bounds(rexp(300)) #> Boundary detection check #> ------------------------ #>   Data range: [0.006111, 5.636] #>  #>   Lower bound: 0 #>   Upper bound: 5.636 #>  #>   Result: Data appears NON-NEGATIVE (lower bound = 0). #>   Recommendation: Use ppc_dens_overlay(bounds = c(0, Inf)) or #>   ppc_ecdf_overlay().  # Proportion data bounded to [0, 1] detect_bounds(rbeta(300, 2, 5)) #> Boundary detection check #> ------------------------ #>   Data range: [0.009824, 0.8503] #>  #>   Lower bound: 0 #>   Upper bound: 1 #>  #>   Result: Data appears to be PROPORTIONS bounded to [0, 1]. #>   Recommendation: Use ppc_dens_overlay(bounds = c(0, 1)) or #>   ppc_ecdf_overlay() to avoid boundary bias in KDE.

what: Detect Discreteness or Point Masses in Data — detect_discrete
title: Detect Discreteness or Point Masses in Data — detect_discrete
text: Applies heuristic Section 2.2 Sailynoja et al. (2025): value relative frequency greater threshold, data flagged potentially discrete containing point masses (e.g., zero-inflation).

title: Detect Discreteness or Point Masses in Data — detect_discrete
code: detect_discrete(y, threshold = 0.02)

title: Detect Discreteness or Point Masses in Data — detect_discrete
text: y Numeric vector observed data. threshold Relative frequency threshold (default 0.02 = 2%). value appearing fraction time flagged.

title: Detect Discreteness or Point Masses in Data — detect_discrete
text: S3 object class "discrete_check" components: is_discrete Logical. TRUE value exceeds threshold. is_mixed Logical. TRUE data appears mixture continuous bulk discrete point masses (values flagged many unique values exist). n_unique Number unique values. n Total number observations. unique_ratio Ratio unique values total observations. flagged_values Named numeric vector values exceeding threshold, relative frequencies values.

title: Detect Discreteness or Point Masses in Data — detect_discrete
text: Sailynoja, T., Johnson, ., Martin, R., & Vehtari, . (2025). Recommendations visual predictive checks Bayesian workflow. arXiv:2503.01509.

title: Detect Discreteness or Point Masses in Data — detect_discrete
code: # Continuous data — should not flag anything detect_discrete(rnorm(200)) #> Discreteness / point-mass check #> -------------------------------- #>   Observations : 200 #>   Unique values: 200 (ratio 1.000) #>   Threshold    : 2.0% #>  #>   Result: No point masses detected. Data appears continuous.  # Discrete counts detect_discrete(rpois(200, lambda = 3)) #> Discreteness / point-mass check #> -------------------------------- #>   Observations : 200 #>   Unique values: 9 (ratio 0.045) #>   Threshold    : 2.0% #>  #>   Result: DISCRETE data detected. #>   Values with relative frequency > threshold: #>     value = 0  (freq = 3.0%) #>     value = 1  (freq = 13.5%) #>     value = 2  (freq = 26.0%) #>     value = 3  (freq = 25.5%) #>     value = 4  (freq = 15.5%) #>     value = 5  (freq = 6.0%) #>     value = 6  (freq = 6.5%) #>     value = 7  (freq = 3.5%) #>   Recommendation: Use ppc_rootogram() or ppc_bars(); #>   avoid KDE-based plots.  # Zero-inflated: mixture of zeros and continuous values y_mixed <- c(rep(0, 60), rnorm(140, mean = 2)) detect_discrete(y_mixed) #> Discreteness / point-mass check #> -------------------------------- #>   Observations : 200 #>   Unique values: 141 (ratio 0.705) #>   Threshold    : 2.0% #>  #>   Result: MIXED / ZERO-INFLATED data detected. #>   Point masses with relative frequency > threshold: #>     value = 0  (freq = 30.0%) #>   Recommendation: Separate discrete/continuous components; #>   consider ppc_rootogram() or a hurdle/mixture model check.

what: Adjust gamma for simultaneous ECDF confidence bands (internal) — .adjust_gamma_optimize
title: Adjust gamma for simultaneous ECDF confidence bands (internal) — .adjust_gamma_optimize
text: Finds smallest gamma simultaneous confidence band P(max_t |F_n(t) - t| <= gamma * sqrt(t(1-t)/n)) >= prob using Dvoretzky-Kiefer-Wolfowitz inequality outer bound binary search Monte Carlo calibration.

title: Adjust gamma for simultaneous ECDF confidence bands (internal) — .adjust_gamma_optimize
code: .adjust_gamma_optimize(n, prob = 0.95, K = NULL, n_sim = 1000L)

title: Adjust gamma for simultaneous ECDF confidence bands (internal) — .adjust_gamma_optimize
text: n Integer. Number observations. prob Nominal coverage probability (e.g. 0.95). K Integer. Number equally-spaced evaluation points (0,1). n_sim Integer. Number Monte Carlo simulations calibration.

title: Adjust gamma for simultaneous ECDF confidence bands (internal) — .adjust_gamma_optimize
text: Numeric scalar gamma.

title: Bootstrap consistency intervals for CEP — .boot_cep
text: Bootstrap consistency intervals CEP

title: Bootstrap consistency intervals for CEP — .boot_cep
code: .boot_cep(y, p, n_quantiles, n_boot, prob)

title: Compute CEP using isotonic regression (PAVA) — .compute_cep
text: Compute CEP using isotonic regression (PAVA)

title: Compute CEP using isotonic regression (PAVA) — .compute_cep
code: .compute_cep(y, p, n_quantiles)

title: Compute CEP using isotonic regression (PAVA) — .compute_cep
text: y Binary integer vector. p Numeric vector predicted probabilities. n_quantiles Integer. Number quantile bins.

title: Cumulative trapezoid integration (internal) — .cumtrapz
text: Computes cumulative integral y x using trapezoid rule. Returns vector length x, first element = 0.

title: Cumulative trapezoid integration (internal) — .cumtrapz
code: .cumtrapz(x, y)

title: Cumulative trapezoid integration (internal) — .cumtrapz
text: x Numeric vector x-coordinates (must sorted). y Numeric vector y-values (length x).

title: Cumulative trapezoid integration (internal) — .cumtrapz
text: Numeric vector cumulative integrals.

title: Compute simultaneous ECDF confidence bands (internal) — .ecdf_bands
text: Returns lower upper pointwise bounds simultaneous coverage approximately prob uniform distribution.

title: Compute simultaneous ECDF confidence bands (internal) — .ecdf_bands
code: .ecdf_bands(z, n, gamma)

title: Compute simultaneous ECDF confidence bands (internal) — .ecdf_bands
text: z Numeric vector evaluation points (0, 1). n Integer. Sample size. gamma Numeric scalar .adjust_gamma_optimize().

title: Compute simultaneous ECDF confidence bands (internal) — .ecdf_bands
text: Data frame columns z, lower, upper.

title: Minimal ggplot2 theme consistent with bayesplot (internal) — .ppcviz_theme

title: Minimal ggplot2 theme consistent with bayesplot (internal) — .ppcviz_theme

what: Compute Visualization PIT Values for a Histogram — pit_histogram
title: Compute Visualization PIT Values for a Histogram — pit_histogram
text: Computes PIT values y respect piecewise-constant density implied histogram y. histogram computed using graphics::hist(), CDF derived cumulative bin densities.

title: Compute Visualization PIT Values for a Histogram — pit_histogram
code: pit_histogram(y, breaks = "Freedman-Diaconis")

title: Compute Visualization PIT Values for a Histogram — pit_histogram
text: y Numeric vector observed data. breaks Breakpoints specification passed graphics::hist(). Default "Freedman-Diaconis". Can string method name, number bins, numeric vector breakpoints.

title: Compute Visualization PIT Values for a Histogram — pit_histogram
text: Numeric vector PIT values \([0,1]\), length y.

title: Compute Visualization PIT Values for a Histogram — pit_histogram
text: Sailynoja, T., Johnson, ., Martin, R., & Vehtari, . (2025). Recommendations visual predictive checks Bayesian workflow. arXiv:2503.01509.

title: Compute Visualization PIT Values for a Histogram — pit_histogram
code: set.seed(42) y <- rnorm(200) pit <- pit_histogram(y) hist(pit, breaks = 20, main = "Histogram PIT — should be ~uniform")

what: Compute Visualization PIT Values for KDE — pit_kde
title: Compute Visualization PIT Values for KDE — pit_kde
text: Computes probability integral transform (PIT) values y respect kernel density estimate (KDE) fitted y . quantifies well KDE density plot represents empirical distribution data.

title: Compute Visualization PIT Values for KDE — pit_kde
code: pit_kde(y, bw = "SJ", kernel = "gaussian", bounds = NULL)

title: Compute Visualization PIT Values for KDE — pit_kde
text: y Numeric vector observed data. bw Bandwidth selector passed stats::density(). Default "SJ" (Sheather-Jones). Can also numeric value. kernel Kernel function passed stats::density(). Default "gaussian". bounds Optional numeric vector length 2 specifying c(lower, upper) bounds. Use NA Inf/-Inf one-sided bounds, e.g. c(0, NA) non-negative data.

title: Compute Visualization PIT Values for KDE — pit_kde
text: Numeric vector PIT values \([0,1]\), length y.

title: Compute Visualization PIT Values for KDE — pit_kde
text: approach : (1) fit KDE using stats::density(), (2) numerically integrate density get CDF via cumulative trapezoid integration, (3) evaluate CDF observation. resulting PIT values uniform \([0,1]\) KDE faithfully represents data. bounds provided, data reflected boundaries fitting KDE ("reflection method"), density cropped bounded region renormalised.

title: Compute Visualization PIT Values for KDE — pit_kde
text: Sailynoja, T., Johnson, ., Martin, R., & Vehtari, . (2025). Recommendations visual predictive checks Bayesian workflow. arXiv:2503.01509.

title: Compute Visualization PIT Values for KDE — pit_kde
code: set.seed(42) y <- rnorm(200) pit <- pit_kde(y) hist(pit, breaks = 20, main = "PIT values — should be ~uniform")   # Bounded data: Beta(2, 5) on [0, 1] y_beta <- rbeta(200, 2, 5) pit_bounded <- pit_kde(y_beta, bounds = c(0, 1)) hist(pit_bounded, breaks = 20)

what: Compute Randomised PIT Values for Quantile Dot Plots — pit_qdotplot
title: Compute Randomised PIT Values for Quantile Dot Plots — pit_qdotplot
text: Computes randomised PIT values data represented quantile dot plot. observation mapped uniform draw range quantile dots overlaps, following Section 2.1 Sailynoja et al. (2025).

title: Compute Randomised PIT Values for Quantile Dot Plots — pit_qdotplot
code: pit_qdotplot(y, n_quantiles = 100L, bw = NULL)

title: Compute Randomised PIT Values for Quantile Dot Plots — pit_qdotplot
text: y Numeric vector observed data. n_quantiles Integer. Number quantile dots (default 100). bw Numeric. Dot radius data scale. NULL (default), uses sqrt(1 / n_quantiles) relative data range, corresponds dots tile cover unit area.

title: Compute Randomised PIT Values for Quantile Dot Plots — pit_qdotplot
text: Numeric vector randomised PIT values \([0,1]\), length y.

title: Compute Randomised PIT Values for Quantile Dot Plots — pit_qdotplot
text: Dot positions n_quantiles empirical quantiles y equally spaced probability points, consistent ggdist::stat_dots(quantiles = n_quantiles) places dots.

title: Compute Randomised PIT Values for Quantile Dot Plots — pit_qdotplot
text: Sailynoja, T., Johnson, ., Martin, R., & Vehtari, . (2025). Recommendations visual predictive checks Bayesian workflow. arXiv:2503.01509.

title: Compute Randomised PIT Values for Quantile Dot Plots — pit_qdotplot
code: set.seed(42) y <- rnorm(200) pit <- pit_qdotplot(y, n_quantiles = 50) hist(pit, breaks = 20, main = "Quantile dot plot PIT")

what: PAVA Calibration Plot for Binary Outcomes — ppc_calibration
title: PAVA Calibration Plot for Binary Outcomes — ppc_calibration
text: Produces calibration plot binary (0/1) posterior predictive checks using isotonic regression (Pool Adjacent Violators Algorithm, PAVA) estimate Conditional Expectation Probabilities (CEP). Consistency intervals obtained via bootstrap resampling.

title: PAVA Calibration Plot for Binary Outcomes — ppc_calibration
code: ppc_calibration(y, yrep, n_quantiles = 10L, prob = 0.9, n_boot = 500L, ...)

title: PAVA Calibration Plot for Binary Outcomes — ppc_calibration
text: y Integer logical vector observed binary outcomes (0/1 FALSE/TRUE). Length n. yrep Numeric matrix posterior predictive draws. Rows = draws, columns = observations. Dimensions: S x n S number posterior draws. n_quantiles Integer. Number quantile bins grouping predicted probabilities applying isotonic regression (default 10). bins give finer resolution noisier estimates. prob Nominal coverage probability consistency intervals (default 0.9). n_boot Integer. Number bootstrap resamples consistency intervals (default 500). ... Currently unused; reserved future arguments.

title: PAVA Calibration Plot for Binary Outcomes — ppc_calibration
text: ggplot object. plot shows predicted probability x-axis, isotonic-regression CEP estimate y-axis, shaded consistency interval, diagonal reference line (perfect calibration).

title: PAVA Calibration Plot for Binary Outcomes — ppc_calibration
text: plot available bayesplot implements methodology Sections 4 Sailynoja et al. (2025).

title: PAVA Calibration Plot for Binary Outcomes — ppc_calibration
text: Sailynoja, T., Johnson, ., Martin, R., & Vehtari, . (2025). Recommendations visual predictive checks Bayesian workflow. arXiv:2503.01509.

title: PAVA Calibration Plot for Binary Outcomes — ppc_calibration
code: set.seed(42) n <- 200 # Well-calibrated predictions true_p <- stats::plogis(rnorm(n)) y <- stats::rbinom(n, 1, true_p) yrep <- do.call(rbind, lapply(seq_len(100), function(i) {   stats::rbinom(n, 1, true_p) })) ppc_calibration(y, yrep)

what: PAVA Calibration Plot for Discrete / Multinomial Outcomes — ppc_calibration_discrete
title: PAVA Calibration Plot for Discrete / Multinomial Outcomes — ppc_calibration_discrete
text: Extends binary calibration plot discrete ordinal outcomes (Section 5 Sailynoja et al. 2025). unique category k y, plots predicted P(Y = k) vs. isotonic-regression CEP category, consistency intervals.

title: PAVA Calibration Plot for Discrete / Multinomial Outcomes — ppc_calibration_discrete
code: ppc_calibration_discrete(   y,   yrep,   n_quantiles = 10L,   prob = 0.9,   n_boot = 500L,   ... )

title: PAVA Calibration Plot for Discrete / Multinomial Outcomes — ppc_calibration_discrete
text: y Integer logical vector observed binary outcomes (0/1 FALSE/TRUE). Length n. yrep Numeric matrix posterior predictive draws. Rows = draws, columns = observations. Dimensions: S x n S number posterior draws. n_quantiles Integer. Number quantile bins grouping predicted probabilities applying isotonic regression (default 10). bins give finer resolution noisier estimates. prob Nominal coverage probability consistency intervals (default 0.9). n_boot Integer. Number bootstrap resamples consistency intervals (default 500). ... Currently unused; reserved future arguments.

title: PAVA Calibration Plot for Discrete / Multinomial Outcomes — ppc_calibration_discrete
text: ggplot object one facet per category.

title: PAVA Calibration Plot for Discrete / Multinomial Outcomes — ppc_calibration_discrete
text: Sailynoja, T., Johnson, ., Martin, R., & Vehtari, . (2025). Recommendations visual predictive checks Bayesian workflow. arXiv:2503.01509.

title: PAVA Calibration Plot for Discrete / Multinomial Outcomes — ppc_calibration_discrete
code: set.seed(42) n <- 200 # Ordinal 0/1/2 outcome true_logit <- rnorm(n) y <- ifelse(true_logit < -0.5, 0L, ifelse(true_logit < 0.5, 1L, 2L)) # Simulate yrep from a noisy version of the true probabilities yrep <- do.call(rbind, lapply(seq_len(100), function(i) {   ifelse(rnorm(n) < -0.5, 0L, ifelse(rnorm(n) < 0.5, 1L, 2L)) })) ppc_calibration_discrete(y, yrep)

title: PAVA Calibration Residual Plot — ppc_calibration_residual
text: Similar ppc_calibration(), plots residuals (CEP - predicted probability) y-axis perfect calibration horizontal line zero. Optionally uses covariate x x-axis diagnose covariate-specific miscalibration.

title: PAVA Calibration Residual Plot — ppc_calibration_residual
code: ppc_calibration_residual(   y,   yrep,   x = NULL,   n_quantiles = 10L,   prob = 0.9,   n_boot = 500L,   ... )

title: PAVA Calibration Residual Plot — ppc_calibration_residual
text: y Integer logical vector observed binary outcomes (0/1 FALSE/TRUE). Length n. yrep Numeric matrix posterior predictive draws. Rows = draws, columns = observations. Dimensions: S x n S number posterior draws. x Optional numeric vector length n. provided, used x-axis variable instead predicted probabilities. Useful visualising calibration function covariate. n_quantiles Integer. Number quantile bins grouping predicted probabilities applying isotonic regression (default 10). bins give finer resolution noisier estimates. prob Nominal coverage probability consistency intervals (default 0.9). n_boot Integer. Number bootstrap resamples consistency intervals (default 500). ... Currently unused; reserved future arguments.

title: PAVA Calibration Residual Plot — ppc_calibration_residual
text: ggplot object.

title: PAVA Calibration Residual Plot — ppc_calibration_residual
text: Sailynoja, T., Johnson, ., Martin, R., & Vehtari, . (2025). Recommendations visual predictive checks Bayesian workflow. arXiv:2503.01509.

title: PAVA Calibration Residual Plot — ppc_calibration_residual
code: set.seed(42) n <- 200 true_p <- stats::plogis(rnorm(n)) y <- stats::rbinom(n, 1, true_p) yrep <- do.call(rbind, lapply(seq_len(100), function(i) {   stats::rbinom(n, 1, true_p) })) ppc_calibration_residual(y, yrep)

title: Automatic PPC Plot Recommender — recommend_ppc
text: Examines data properties (discreteness, boundaries) optionally tests whether KDE visualization faithful data, returns data-driven recommendation posterior predictive check (PPC) plot function use. Recommendations follow decision logic Sailynoja et al. (2025).

title: Automatic PPC Plot Recommender — recommend_ppc
code: recommend_ppc(y, yrep = NULL)

title: Automatic PPC Plot Recommender — recommend_ppc
text: y Numeric vector observed data. yrep Optional numeric matrix posterior predictive draws (rows = draws, columns = observations). provided y continuous, runs check_viz() test whether KDE appropriate.

title: Automatic PPC Plot Recommender — recommend_ppc
text: S3 object class "ppc_recommendation" components: recommended Character vector recommended function names. avoid Character vector functions avoid. reason Character scalar explaining recommendation. data_type One "continuous", "discrete", "mixed", "binary". bounds Detected boundary information (bounds_check object). discrete Discreteness detection result (discrete_check object). kde_gof viz_gof object KDE tested, otherwise NULL.

title: Automatic PPC Plot Recommender — recommend_ppc
text: Sailynoja, T., Johnson, ., Martin, R., & Vehtari, . (2025). Recommendations visual predictive checks Bayesian workflow. arXiv:2503.01509.

title: Automatic PPC Plot Recommender — recommend_ppc
code: set.seed(42)  # Continuous unbounded recommend_ppc(rnorm(300)) #> PPC plot recommendation #> ======================= #>   Data type: CONTINUOUS #>  #>   Reason: #>     Data appears CONTINUOUS and UNBOUNDED. ppc_dens_overlay() is #>     generally appropriate. Provide `yrep` to additionally test KDE #>     faithfulness.  #>  #>   RECOMMENDED: #>     + ppc_dens_overlay() #>     + ppc_ecdf_overlay() #>   # Non-negative (exponential) recommend_ppc(rexp(300)) #> PPC plot recommendation #> ======================= #>   Data type: CONTINUOUS #>  #>   Reason: #>     Data appears NON-NEGATIVE with a hard lower bound at 0. Use #>     boundary-corrected KDE with bounds = c(0, Inf) to prevent #>     density mass at negative values.  #>  #>   RECOMMENDED: #>     + ppc_dens_overlay(bounds = c(0, Inf)) #>     + ppc_ecdf_overlay() #>  #>   AVOID: #>     - ppc_dens_overlay()  # without bounds #>   # Proportions recommend_ppc(rbeta(300, 2, 5)) #> PPC plot recommendation #> ======================= #>   Data type: CONTINUOUS #>  #>   Reason: #>     Data appears to be PROPORTIONS bounded to [0, 1]. Use #>     boundary-corrected KDE with bounds = c(0, 1) to avoid density #>     leakage outside the valid range.  #>  #>   RECOMMENDED: #>     + ppc_dens_overlay(bounds = c(0, 1)) #>     + ppc_ecdf_overlay() #>  #>   AVOID: #>     - ppc_dens_overlay()  # without bounds #>   # Discrete counts recommend_ppc(rpois(300, lambda = 3)) #> PPC plot recommendation #> ======================= #>   Data type: DISCRETE #>  #>   Reason: #>     Data is DISCRETE with 11 unique values. KDE-based density plots #>     will misrepresent the distribution. Use rootogram or bar plots #>     for small discrete spaces, or ECDF-based plots for larger ones.  #>  #>   RECOMMENDED: #>     + ppc_rootogram() #>     + ppc_bars() #>  #>   AVOID: #>     - ppc_dens_overlay() #>     - ppc_dots() #>   # Zero-inflated recommend_ppc(c(rep(0, 80), rnorm(220, mean = 2))) #> PPC plot recommendation #> ======================= #>   Data type: MIXED #>  #>   Reason: #>     Data appears MIXED / ZERO-INFLATED. Point masses were detected #>     alongside a continuous bulk. Separate the discrete and #>     continuous components, or use ppc_rootogram() for count-like #>     data.  #>  #>   RECOMMENDED: #>     + ppc_rootogram() #>     + ppc_ecdf_overlay(discrete = TRUE) #>  #>   AVOID: #>     - ppc_dens_overlay() #>   # Binary recommend_ppc(sample(0:1, 300, replace = TRUE)) #> PPC plot recommendation #> ======================= #>   Data type: BINARY #>  #>   Reason: #>     Data is BINARY (only 0 and 1). Use PAVA calibration plots to #>     assess probability calibration. Density-based and ECDF plots #>     are not appropriate for binary outcomes.  #>  #>   RECOMMENDED: #>     + ppc_calibration() #>     + ppc_calibration_residual() #>  #>   AVOID: #>     - ppc_dens_overlay() #>     - ppc_ecdf_overlay() #>

what: Goodness-of-Fit Test for Visualization PIT Values — viz_gof
title: Goodness-of-Fit Test for Visualization PIT Values — viz_gof
text: Tests whether set PIT values (pit_kde(), pit_histogram(), pit_qdotplot()) consistent uniform distribution \([0,1]\), using simultaneous ECDF confidence bands. Non-uniformity indicates visualization misleading — faithfully represent distribution data.

title: Goodness-of-Fit Test for Visualization PIT Values — viz_gof
code: viz_gof(pit, prob = 0.95, K = NULL, n_sim = 1000L)

title: Goodness-of-Fit Test for Visualization PIT Values — viz_gof
text: pit Numeric vector PIT values \([0,1]\) (e.g. pit_kde(), pit_histogram(), pit_qdotplot()). prob Nominal simultaneous coverage probability confidence band (default 0.95). K Integer. Number evaluation points \((0,1)\) ECDF comparison. NULL, uses min(length(pit), 1000). n_sim Integer. Number Monte Carlo simulations used calibrate band. Default 1000. Increase smoother results.

title: Goodness-of-Fit Test for Visualization PIT Values — viz_gof
text: S3 object class "viz_gof" components: pit input PIT values. passes Logical. TRUE ECDF lies entirely within confidence band (visualization passes GoF test). K Number evaluation points used. prob Nominal coverage probability. ecdf_values Numeric vector ECDF values K points. z Evaluation points (expected quantiles uniformity). lower Lower band boundary evaluation point. upper Upper band boundary evaluation point. max_deviation Maximum |ECDF(z) - z| evaluation points. gamma Calibrated gamma used band.

title: Goodness-of-Fit Test for Visualization PIT Values — viz_gof
text: simultaneous bands use Sailynoja, Burkner & Vehtari (2022) calibration method: scaled Brownian bridge envelope \([t \pm \gamma \sqrt{t(1-t)/n}]\) \(\gamma\) determined Monte Carlo simulation achieve desired simultaneous coverage.

title: Goodness-of-Fit Test for Visualization PIT Values — viz_gof
text: Sailynoja, T., Johnson, ., Martin, R., & Vehtari, . (2025). Recommendations visual predictive checks Bayesian workflow. arXiv:2503.01509. Sailynoja, T., Burkner, P.-C., & Vehtari, . (2022). Graphical test discrete uniformity faster alternative Kolmogorov-Smirnov test. Statistics Computing, 32, 32.

title: Goodness-of-Fit Test for Visualization PIT Values — viz_gof
code: set.seed(42) # Should pass: PIT from a well-calibrated KDE of normal data y <- rnorm(300) pit <- pit_kde(y) result <- viz_gof(pit) print(result) #> [OK] Visualization GoF test PASSES (prob = 95%, max deviation = 0.0250) #>     The visualization appears faithful to the data distribution. plot(result)   # Should potentially fail: KDE on bounded Beta data without bounds correction y_beta <- rbeta(300, 2, 5) pit_bad <- pit_kde(y_beta)  # no bounds = boundary bias result_bad <- viz_gof(pit_bad) print(result_bad) #> [OK] Visualization GoF test PASSES (prob = 95%, max deviation = 0.0350) #>     The visualization appears faithful to the data distribution.
